title: Commercial Roofing in Delta Township, MI | Commercial Roofing Contractors of Lansing

html:
Delta Township Runs Two Different Roofs: Assembly-Plant Membrane and Big-Box Retail

Delta Township holds two commercial roof categories that don't behave the same way and shouldn't be maintained the same way. On one end there's the GM Lansing Delta Township assembly plant, a low-slope membrane roof measured in acres, built to shed weather over a working production floor. On the other end is the Lansing Mall corridor along West Saginaw Highway, a run of big-box and strip retail roofs carrying rooftop HVAC, signage structures, and decades of patchwork repair. We work both, and we tell owners up front which category their building falls into before we talk about systems or pricing.

What an Assembly-Plant Roof Actually Has to Support

A plant roof the size of the Delta Township assembly complex isn't one membrane decision, it's dozens of small ones repeated over a huge field. Paint booth exhaust stacks, makeup-air units, dust collection curbs, and conveyor penetrations all interrupt the field membrane, and each interruption is a place water finds first. When we walk a roof like this, we're not scanning for one bad spot, we're mapping every curb, pitch pan, and expansion joint because that's where 90 percent of manufacturing roof leaks actually start.

Owners of large industrial roofs often assume a leak means the whole membrane is failing. Usually it doesn't. It means one flashing detail around one piece of rooftop equipment failed, and the fix is targeted, not a full reroof. The risk is waiting long enough that water tracks along the deck and shows up fifty feet from the actual source, which turns a two-hundred-dollar flashing repair into a section of wet insulation that has to be cut out and replaced.

Retail Roofs on the Lansing Mall Corridor

The retail roofs along the Lansing Mall corridor carry a different set of problems. These are older EPDM and built-up systems in a lot of cases, with rooftop units added and swapped out over the years by different mechanical contractors who didn't always flash their curbs back to spec. We see a lot of ponding water on these roofs because the original slope was minimal to begin with and years of re-roofing over old insulation flattened it out further.

For a retail owner or property manager, the calculation is different than for a manufacturer. Downtime isn't the main cost, it's the tenant relationship. A leak over a sales floor during business hours is a liability problem before it's a roofing problem. We schedule retail work around store hours where we can and we're upfront when a repair needs a temporary interior protection plan for the tenant, because that's a conversation the owner should be having before the crew shows up, not after.

Freeze-Thaw Cycling on Delta Township's Flat Roofs

Mid-Michigan winters put every flat roof in Delta Township through repeated freeze-thaw cycles, and the damage compounds in ways that aren't visible from the ground. Water gets into a seam or a flashing lap during a January thaw, refreezes overnight, and expands the gap a little more each time. By March that small seam separation has become an active leak point. The failure patterns we see most often on these roofs run in a fairly predictable order:

  • Membrane seams and laps that have opened from repeated expansion and contraction
  • Pitch pans around conduit and pipe penetrations cracking from thermal movement
  • Ponded water refreezing at low points and stressing the membrane surface
  • Edge metal and coping fasteners backing out as the substrate moves
  • Roof drains and scuppers icing over and backing water up onto the field
  • Insulation saturation under membrane that's been walked or trafficked repeatedly

None of these show up as a dramatic failure right away. They show up as a slow-growing stain on a ceiling tile eighteen months later, after the damage is already done to the deck underneath.

Coordinating Work Around a Running Plant

Roof work at a facility like the assembly plant has to fit around a production schedule that doesn't stop for weather or convenience. That means staging materials so they don't block dock doors, sequencing crane and lift work around shift changes, and being able to close out a section of roof completely before a shift starts rather than leaving it open overnight. We plan the work in sections for exactly this reason, so a partial-day rain event never leaves an open roof over an active production line.

This is also where a lot of outside contractors underbid the job. Anyone can quote square footage of membrane. Fewer contractors account for the logistics of moving crews and materials around an operating industrial site without disrupting it, and that logistics gap is usually where change orders and delays come from later.

Recover Versus Tear-Off: The Real Decision Point

For both the plant roof and the retail roofs on this corridor, the recover-versus-tear-off decision comes down to what's under the existing membrane. If the insulation is dry and the deck is sound, a recover system can add fifteen to twenty years of service life at a fraction of tear-off cost. If there's trapped moisture, recovering over it just seals in a problem that keeps degrading the insulation from underneath, and the owner ends up paying for the recover now and the tear-off later anyway.

We don't make that call by eyeballing the roof from the parking lot. A moisture scan and a handful of core cuts tell us what's actually happening under the membrane, and that data is what the recommendation is built on, not a sales target.

Questions Delta Township Owners Ask Us

Does a manufacturing roof need a different membrane than a retail roof?

Not necessarily a different membrane type, but a different detailing approach. A plant roof has far more penetrations and curbs per square foot, so the flashing work matters more than the field membrane choice itself.

Can you work around production shifts at the assembly plant?

Yes. We section the work and coordinate staging and crane access around the plant's schedule so an open roof is never left exposed overnight over an active line.

Why do the retail roofs near Lansing Mall keep getting patch repairs instead of being replaced?

In a lot of cases the membrane still has usable life left and a targeted repair is the right call. We only recommend replacement when the underlying insulation or deck is compromised, not because a roof has a few years on it.

How often should a big industrial roof like this be inspected?

Twice a year at minimum, once before winter and once after, plus a walk after any significant hail or wind event. That cadence catches seam and flashing issues before a freeze-thaw cycle turns them into leaks.

What does a moisture scan actually tell us before a recover decision?

It maps where water has already gotten into the insulation so we can decide whether the whole field qualifies for a recover or whether specific sections need to be cut out and replaced first.
